dialogue.utterance: Units 3 and 5 respond to 135 E. Broad Street for a Sick Person with altered mental status.
Hi, I'm Anne, an EMT with the rescue squad. What can you tell me about her condition?
She was found unresponsive and lying in her bed.
Is she responding to loud voice or touch?
No, she didn't move or speak.
Can you prompt any response? Squeeze my hand if she feels it.
She is completely unresponsive with a GCS of 3-eyes, verbal, and motor all zero.
Airway is patent with no signs of obstruction or unusual positioning.
She's breathing at 20 bpm, with equal chest rise and no signs of distress.
Blood pressure is 90/56, pulse is 108. She's hot, pale, and clammy with weak pulses.
Family, could you share her recent health issues or changes?
She was told she had a UTI and hasn't been eating or drinking.
Has she had a high temperature or chills?
She's running a fever. She's in hospice for breast cancer and had shingles.
What medications is she currently on?
Amiodarone for her heart, Pravastatin for cholesterol, and Prilosec for her stomach.
When was the last time she ate or drank?
Last night, I believe.
Has she been around anyone sick, or traveled recently?
No, she's been too unwell to do much of anything.
Pulse is 108 with regular rhythm.
Respiratory rate is 20, even and without distress.
Her blood pressure is 90/56, still low.
Oxygen saturation is 97% on her home 2 LPM nostril cannula.
Blood glucose is 150.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. Attaching 4-lead monitoring now.
Her temperature is 103.1°-she has a fever.
Is she in pain, to your knowledge?
No, she hasn't mentioned any pain or discomfort.
Skin feels hot, pale, and clammy. No rashes or bruising noted.
Pupils are equal at 2mm, round, and reactive to light. There's a large healing shingles rash on the left side of her face.
Bilateral clear breath sounds. Heart tones are S1 and S2 with no murmur. 12-lead EKG is showing nonspecific ST changes and sinus tach.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and not distended.
No deformity or trauma to the extremities. Bilateral mastectomy noted.
We're placing her on 2 LPM oxygen as per her home order. Partner, get ready for IV access.
4-lead and 12-lead EKG in place. Also hooking her on 2 LPM oxygen.
Let's attempt IV placement. Partner, ready the bag for fluids.
IV placed on the right hand with 22-gauge. Giving 250 mL normal saline as her first bolus.
With altered mental status, hypotension, fever, and low output, she meets sepsis criteria. We are following the hypotension/shock protocol.
After IV, BP improved to 101/68, pulse down a little to 107.
Her BP dropped to 72/37, but no new signs of respiratory or circulatory distress.
BP improved to 124/77 with pulse at 112. SpO2 is now at 99%.
Her latest BP is 121/70 and SpO2 is 100%. Respiratory rate is 21.
She now has a GCS of 9 and responds to verbal stimuli.
We are transporting her to Chippenham Hospital. Partner, prepare for RN documentation due to her altered status.
She's stabilized. Moving to the stairchair and preparing to secure for transport.
Patient has IV fluids running, is receiving 2 LPM home oxygen, and her mental status has improved.
Collected RN signature upon arrival at hospital due to her unresponsiveness and ongoing conditions.
Her prior medications include breast cancer meds, diabetes meds, and heart condition drugs.
All documentation is in place and ready to transfer when we arrive at EC.
She is safely moved from the stairchair to the stretcher and secured for transport.
Transporting to Chippenham on direct route as per protocol.
Her breathing is at 23. She remains stable with ongoing monitoring.
She responds to familiar voice but not speaking yet. Still at GCS 9.
Skin is still hot and pale. No new abnormalities noted.
Pupils remain equally reactive. Shingles rash looks the same. No signs of JVD.
IV is patent and delivering smoothly. Weaker pulses remain but are regular.
She is secured and stable for transport. ER has been advised of her IV and flag for altered mental status.
Oxygen is maintained at 2 LPM. Patient will be transferred at Chippenham.
BP at 124/77, pulse at 112. Oxygen is at 99% on monitor.
Latest vitals: 121/70 BP, 100% O2 saturation, and 21 respiratory rate.
All systems are secured for safe transport.
She has breast cancer history, a recent UTI, and a healing shingles rash. No prior active pain.
Equipment and patient secured per standard. No further movement protocols planned.
ER was alerted by_phone earlier. Full clinical handover awaits arrival.
Patient is stable. No new medical actions needed before reaching ER.
RN is managing ongoing documentation. All care transition notes ready for ER integration.
Energizing the unit for direct transport. Transport process complete.
Arrival expected soon. ER has comprehensive patient info and logs.
SPO2 remains stable at 100%. No acute cardiac concerns.
IV fluids, home O2, and sepsis care are in progress.
Final patient status remains unchanged, all transporting safely.
Handoff to ER is prepped. ER team is aware of key clinical concerns.
Ambulance departure secure with no incident noted at this point.
ER team is awaiting arrival. No delays during transport symptoms.
No changes in IV status. Patient has no additional need for drugs now.
All medical and transport records accounted for upon arrival in ER.
Vital signs remain stable. Patient in full transport condition.
ER team is confirming readiness for incoming patient. All set.
No deviation from transport norm. All elements in place.
Patient ready for delivery. Transfer details complete.
Recheck SPO2, it's still at 98%, after documented drop from baseline.
ETCO2 is in range, 35-40 with a square waveform. Added monitoring now.
Her skin remains hot. Capillary refill but no pain reported.
Initial point has null pain value. She doesn't report pain to family or EMS.
She has uterine cancer as part of her broader medical history.
Full documentation transferred with final preparation for ER turnover.
Patient relocated as ECG and oxygen levels hold. No acute needs.
Any needed interventions during transport have been recorded and handled.
Transfer of care is scheduled for immediate post-arrival.
BGL measurement taken during initial vitals. No changes so far.
Final steps toward Chippenham ahead. ER is briefed and on watch.
All remaining transport aspects in compliance upon completion.
No further active medical steps. Patient destiny ahead at ER.
